content: N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization is a {{c1::liquid embolic agent}}.
N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization rapidly polymerizes on contact with {{c1::ionic substances such as blood or saline}}.
In N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization, NBCA is mixed with {{c1::an oil-based contrast agent}} to {{c2::adjust the viscosity and regulate the polymerization rate}}.

What solution should be used to flush the microcatheter before N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization?

{{c1::5% dextrose}}
Before N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization, normal saline should be avoided because it can {{c1::induce premature NBCA polymerization}}.
N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization is especially useful for {{c1::controlling arterial bleeding from small-caliber or tortuous vessels}}.
A major complication of N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization is {{c1::nontarget embolization}} and can cause tissue necrosis or ischemia.
During N-butyl cyanoacrylate (NBCA) glue embolization, understanding vascular anatomy and hemodynamics is essential to {{c1::minimize complications}}.

{{c1::Early opacification of the portal vein}}
